name: Chavacano (Zamboangueño)

ancestors.notes: Spanish is the lexifier; the orthography is the Spanish one.

sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.title: Chavacano
sources.pages: Alphabet, seseo, non-lenition of intervocalic /b d ɡ/, yeísmo.

sources.author: Lipski, John M.
sources.title: Modern Spanish once-removed in Philippine creole Spanish: the case of Zamboangueño
sources.pages: pp. 93-99: seseo throughout, no /θ/; ⟨j⟩ and ⟨g⟩ before front vowels realised [h]; yeísmo (⟨ll⟩ = [j]); ⟨v⟩ = [b]; non-lenition of intervocalic /b d ɡ/ (retained stops, unlike peninsular Spanish).
sources.notes: Zamboangueño phonology and its divergence from Spanish; documents the retention of stop /b d ɡ/ that motivates the non-lenited allophone map.

sources.author: Forman, Michael L.
sources.title: Zamboangueño texts with grammatical analysis: a study of Philippine Creole Spanish
sources.pages: Ch. 2 (phonology): five-vowel system /a e i o u/; preverbal TMA particles ya (perfective), ta (imperfective/progressive), ay~hay (future/irrealis); negation with no.
sources.notes: Foundational descriptive grammar and text corpus; source for the TMA-particle syntax and the practical Spanish-based orthography used in the gold register.

sources.author: Lipski, John M.
sources.title: The place of Chabacano in the Philippine linguistic identity
sources.pages: Zamboangueño as the largest and most vital Chabacano variety; lexicon predominantly Spanish with Philippine (Cebuano/Hiligaynon/Tagalog) substrate morphosyntax.
sources.notes: Situates Zamboangueño among the Chabacano varieties (Caviteño, Ternateño, Ermitaño); support for the substrate ancestry weighting.

notes: Zamboangueño Chavacano, a Spanish-lexifier creole of the Philippines, written with the Spanish alphabet. Seseo throughout (⟨c⟩ before e/i, ⟨z⟩ = /s/; no /θ/); ⟨j⟩ and ⟨g⟩ before e/i = /h/~/x/; yeísmo (⟨ll⟩ = /j/, ʎ conservative); ⟨v⟩ = /b/. Unlike most Spanish varieties, /b d ɡ/ keep their stop realisation between vowels (no approximant lenition), so the allophone map is deliberately non-lenited. Acute-accented vowels ⟨á é í ó ú⟩ mark orthographic stress on final-stressed verb roots (ya comprá, ta llamá) and are mapped to their base vowel qualities; the engine carries no lexical-stress model for this creole, so stress placement is not phonemically rendered.
